kept.text:
米軍AI軍事スタックの再編へ
公式発表では、SpaceX、OpenAI、Google、NVIDIA、Reflection、Microsoft、AWS、Oracleの8社が対象です。クラウド、計算基盤、通信・宇宙インフラまで含めた多層構造、ここで重要になるのが、AADA（Autonomous Adversarial Defense Architecture）の発想です。
つまり、AIを軍に導入するというのは、AI同士の攻防・検証・修復を機密ネットワーク上で継続的に回すための基盤整備と見るべきです。DARPAのAI Cyber Challengeも、重要ソフトウェアを守るためにAIとサイバーセキュリティの専門家を結集し、自動的な脆弱性発見・修復を競う取り組みとして進められてきました。

よって、AIを単なる意思決定支援ツールとして使うだけではなく、AI Red Teamが継続的に攻撃シミュレーションを行い、AI Blue Teamが検知・防御・封じ込めを行い、AI Validatorが防御の有効性を検証し、AI Repair Agentが脆弱性や設定不備を自動修復する。

一方でAnthropicが外れている点は、軍事AIにおける「能力・倫理・統制」の衝突を象徴しています。AIの軍事利用は、もはや技術論ではなく、サプライチェーン、同盟、ガバナンス、そして人間がどこで最終判断を担うのかという統制設計の問題になっています。

今後の軍事優位は、「AIを使う軍」ではなく、「AI同士の攻防ループを統制できる軍」に移っていくと思います。

kept.text:
I still remember when we got a call in 2022 from an existing investor about the @GalaxEye team, who were building Earth observation tech. I kid you not, it took us just a day to get back and say we'd like to support them.

Not because we understood the tech deeply or could see the future. But because tech like this hadn't been attempted by an Indian startup before. We've always depended on solutions from other countries, often blindly assuming they're the better choice. The past 2-3 years, with everything happening geopolitically,  have made it clear why depending on anyone externally for a critical capability like Earth observation is a bad idea.

So what is GalaxEye building?
Most satellites give you one kind of view. OptoSAR offers something different. They've built a single payload that combines an X-band radar (sees through clouds, works at night) with a 7-band multispectral camera (sees details our eyes can't), mounted on one bench. The result: continuous, all-weather, day-or-night imagery of Earth.

Their first satellite, called Drishti, launches tomorrow on @SpaceX's Falcon 9 as part of the CAS500-2 rideshare mission.

We've heard enough narratives about India being the next spacetech destination. It's time for more Indian startups to prove it.

Please wish the GalaxEye team the very best! And do watch the stream tomorrow (link in the comments).🚀

quoted_tweet.text:
Elon said something that stuck with me. 

He said the hardest part of being him isn't building the rockets, it's caring about everyone he can't save.

That single line changes how you see him.

Most people think the secret to Musk is intelligence. Or work ethic. Or risk tolerance. They miss the real thing.

His edge is that he has a heart.

In a world optimized for cold optimization, where every CEO is trained to maximize shareholder value and minimize emotional exposure, Musk does the opposite. He builds what moves him. He fights for what matters to him.

A 15 year old girl named Liv Perrotto designed a Shiba Inu plush in 30 minutes. She had cancer. Stage 4. The plush was her contribution to Polaris Dawn, the SpaceX mission that flew higher than any private spaceflight in history. She named it Asteroid.

The plush flew. It became the mission's zero g indicator. The first thing that floated when they hit space.

Liv died in January.

Before she died she wrote eight wishes for Elon. The eighth one was simple. Make Asteroid the official mascot of SpaceX.

She knew she wouldn't see it happen. She wrote it anyway.

When the request reached Elon, he didn't have to respond. He's the richest man alive. He gets thousands of dying wishes. Most go unanswered, that's just math.

He answered this one. He said yes.

Asteroid is now the SpaceX mascot. Because a 15 year old girl drew a dog and asked the most powerful man in the world to remember her.

This is the part nobody understands about Musk. He could have ignored it. The optimal capital allocation move was to ignore it. The brand calculation said ignore it. The lawyers said ignore it.

He didn't ignore it.

People debate whether he's a genius or a clown, a hero or a villain, a savior or a scammer. They miss the point entirely. The reason he keeps winning isn't his intelligence. It's that he hasn't optimized away his humanity.

The other tech founders are smarter at certain things. They have better processes. Better PR teams. Better political instincts. None of them would have made Asteroid the mascot of SpaceX. Most of them couldn't tell you the name of a child who died of cancer last year.

That's the gap. That's the moat. That's why he beats them all.

In a world that rewards detachment, Musk's superpower is that he still feels things. The Tesla mission was personal. The SpaceX mission is personal. Neuralink is personal because his son was non-verbal until eight. Even the Twitter purchase was personal, his obsession with free speech tied to his own censoring.

Every project is downstream of something he actually cares about.

That's why he can work 100 hours a week for 20 years without burning out the way normal people do. Burnout comes from doing things that don't match your values. He's never had to do that.

Liv didn't get to see her plush become the SpaceX mascot. But she wrote it down before she died, and the most powerful man alive said yes, because somewhere underneath the rockets and the satellites and the AI companies and the trillion-dollar valuations, he's still the kid who cried watching cartoons.

Most people lose this by 30. They call it growing up. It's actually atrophy.

@elonmusk kept it.

That's the whole secret.

#SpaceX官方吉祥物  #Asteroid 完整催泪故事

这只温柔的太空柴犬Asteroid，是全世界最浪漫、最心酸的太空吉祥物，背后藏着一个女孩奔赴星辰的一生。

女孩名叫莉芙 Liv，出生于2010年，从小痴迷星空、火箭、SpaceX，是马斯克最忠实的小粉丝。

年仅13岁时，莉芙确诊罕见骨癌，病痛日夜折磨她，化疗、手术从未停下。身处病痛黑暗里，支撑她活下去唯一的光，就是仰望太空，梦想亲眼看见火箭升空。

她最大的心愿：亲手设计一只玩偶，跟着火箭飞上太空。

忍着身体剧痛，莉芙一笔一画，画出了一只软乎乎、带着太空气息的浅棕柴犬，给它取名 #Asteroid 小行星。
她希望这只小狗，代替自己穿梭宇宙，漫游星海。

2024年，SpaceX知晓了这个患病女孩的心愿，破例将Asteroid柴犬带上Polaris Dawn私人载人航天任务。
火箭冲破大气层，进入零重力宇宙，小小的柴犬稳稳漂浮在船舱里，真正抵达了莉芙向往的星河。

这只柴犬不只是普通玩偶，更是太空任务里零重力标识物，代表温柔与希望。

上天之后，莉芙无比幸福，她时常看着太空传回的画面，笑着说：我的小狗在天上陪着星星了。

可病魔依旧无情，2026年初，年仅16岁的莉芙，永远离开了这个世界。

她离世前留下最后一个小小的遗愿：
希望我设计的Asteroid柴犬，能成为SpaceX正式官方吉祥物。

无数网友被故事打动，纷纷带着女孩的心愿，一次次艾特马斯克。

2026年4月19日，马斯克亲自回应，温柔应允：
Ok :)

简简单单两个字符，圆满了少女一生最后的期盼。

从此，#Asteroid 太空柴犬正式成为SpaceX独一无二、饱含爱意的官方吉祥物。

它飘荡在浩瀚宇宙，替没能长大的小女孩，永远望着人间，守护星空。

一颗小行星陨落人间，一只柴犬永远留在太空。

世间再无莉芙，星河永存Asteroid。

kept.text:
A "fraqueza" do Libertarianismo é que é melhor viver os valores dele do que defender ele.

O contrário também é verdade. A "força" do socialismo e do estatismo é que é melhor defender ele do que viver os valores dele.

E por isso tem tanta gente defendendo estado, e pouca gente defendendo libertarianismo. Incentivos.

Exemplos: Tallis Gomes. Ele é libertário, e vive os valores disso. Foi viver a vida dele, ganhou muito dinheiro, sucesso e fama, tem uma empresa crescendo, viaja o mundo, tem coisas boas, se diverte e tem uma boa vida espiritual.

Isso porque ele tá cagando de convencer as outras pessoas. Ele fala o que ele acha, e pronto. Se ele investisse pesadamente o tempo e dinheiro dele em convencer você, a vida dele pioraria significativamente.

Outro exemplo: Hélio Beltrão. Muita gente nem sabe que ele é libertário porque ele também não fica falando disso pra todo lado o tempo todo. Muita gente também não sabe que ele ganhou uma quantia colossal de dinheiro operando no mercado financeiro usando teoria de escola austríaca. Inclusive, foi com esse dinheiro que ele fundou e rodou o Instituto Mises, a um grande prejuízo financeiro pessoal, e dor de cabeça no processo. 

Se ele tivesse tornado defender libertarianismo e escola austríaca como a atividade principal dele na vida, não teria conseguido. O que fez dar certo foi que ele primeiro viveu os valores e ideias de ser um libertário, ganhou um belo dinheiro com isso, e colocou uma parte disso pra defender a ideia. 

O Elon Musk também é um exemplo disso. Ele não tá interessado em convencer você. Ele tá interessado em fazer foguete dar ré. É muito mais divertido.

E a lista contiua. Tem muitos, muitos libertários como CEOs e fundadores de empresas por aí que você nem sabem que são ancaps. Mas são. Inclusive no Brasil.

E eles não vão parar a vida deles pra explicar isso pros outros.

Porque não vale a pena.

Eles ganham MUITO mais fazendo o deles, do que indo convencer quem odeia eles, gastando dinheiro para enfrentar inimigos, se tornando um alvo e sofrendo consequências. 

Agora olhe quem de fato colocou a vida em defender libertarianismo. Dá dinheiro? Te digo de dez anos fazendo isso: se não der prejuízo tá ótimo. Existe uma carreira nisso? Não. É divertido e prazeroso? Também não. Você vai enfrentar inimigos estatistas na direita, socialistas na esquerda, e também internamente entre liberais e libertários. 

Não é só a minha experiência pessoal. É a de todo mundo que eu conheço que entrou em fazer isso. No mundo todo.

Viver os valores de liberdade, buscar sucesso, buscar felicidade, resolver problemas do mundo como uma forma de sucesso financeiro, significado e propósito, viver sua vida espiritual e cuidar da sua família e filhos, isso é de fato bom, recompensador e positivo pessoalmente.

A conclusão é que a forma mais eficiente e sustentável de defender libertarianismo é como um hobby, aquele projeto extra que você faz nas horas que sobram com o dinheiro que sobra.

Aliás, te convido a fazer um experimento. Procure uma pessoa que você consegue ter uma boa conversa sincera, e que você considera financeiramente, espiritualmente e psicologicamente bem sucedida. Pergunte os valores dela, as crenças e as visões de sociedade dela. Você vai descobrir na maioria das vezes que ela é instintivamente libertária. Claro, vai ter umas contradições ali e aqui, mas o núcleo está lá. 

Vai ser uma pessoa que defende mérito e trabalho duro. Uma pessoa que não quer mandar na vida dos outros, e que não aceita que mandem nela. Uma pessoa que aceita que outros discordem dela, desde que não envolva ela em algo que ela não quer. Vai ser uma pessoa que foca primeiro nos problemas dela e da família dela, e daquilo que ela escolhe participar. 

Ela espalha libertarianismo indiretamente. Por exemplo, por vida. Tanto é que eu diria que uns 80% dos libertários que eu conheço, quando descobriram libertarianismo pensaram: Ah, então isso que eu fui criado pra achar e pensei a vida toda que era o certo tem um nome. 

Agora veja o outro lado, o estatismo e o socialismo.

Dá MUITO dinheiro defender isso. Você pode fazer uma carreira inteira, uma empresa gigante, institutos enormes, defendendo que tem que sim ter estado, imposto, políticos, etc.

Exemplos? Você já tem uns 5 na sua cabeça agora. Ligue a TV. Veja um debate político. Veja os grandes empresários do país. 

Ouso dizer que a melhor decisão de carreira e financeira que você pode tomar é decidir ficar bom em defender o estado. 

Sempre vai ter um grupo disposto a te dar muito dinheiro para dizer que eles precisam de ainda mais dinheiro e poder. Você nem precisa estar certo, é só falar qualquer coisa, sorrir e acenar.

Pergunte pra Daniela Lima. É fácil.

Pergunte pro professor concursado da UF do seu estado, que não dá aula direito a 10 anos e só puxa greve. Pergunte pro presidente do sindicato, desembargador, pro fiscal do governo. Pergunte pro empresário amigo de político.

Como essa gente acha certo tomar o dinheiro dos outros, você tem um fundo quase infinito de dinheiro pra bancar propagandista, apoiador, militante, etc. 

Agora olhe a vida deles. Se é uma vida boa, é porque são hipócritas. Porque pegam o dinheiro e a fama e vão viver o capitalismo. Vão consumir, viajar, comprar uma casa bonita para a família e celebrar enquanto os outros sofrem as consequências.

Uma variante igualmente hipócrita disso é aquele que fugiu do Brasil pra um país muito mais livre e capitalista, e continua defendendo socialismo, indo na embaixada do Brasil na Austrália pra votar no PT.

Mas essa vida boa é uma aparência. Se você for ver o mental, não é sólido. Tem um bando de transtornos ali. Se for ver a família, ou os relacionamentos, vai ver instabilidade, maluquice, sofrimento.

Agora veja quem abraça mesmo estatismo e esquerdismo. Veja quem vive mesmo o "eu estou certo e isso me dá o direito de me impor em cima dos outros". Veja quem vive mesmo o "eu sou uma vítima da sociedade".

É uma vida lixo. É uma vida que se desmonta inteira, que deixa vítimas pra todo lado, que é uma vergonha até mesmo para o movimento que ele defende.

Pega o maluco lá que toda hora tá num barco pra Gaza, pegou sarna, é preso pra todo lado, abandona a família e agora é acusado de assédio. É disso que eu estou falando.

Olhe o ativista médio dos partidos de extrema esquerda. Sovaco peludo, físico raquítico ou obeso, franja do pai ausente, abuso de substâncias, transtornos mentais.

Diferente do hipócrita, ele de fato absorveu os valores da filosofia que ele defende. E isso visivelmente destrói ele por dentro.

Algo similar acontece com o estatista de direita. Aquele que defende autoritarismo, planejamento central, força, moralidade imposta. Aquele que acha que fazer alguém ajoelhar na igreja com um fuzil mirado na cabeça vai criar um Cristão.

Olhe a vida dele. A família dele. A aparência dele. É algo ou patético, onde parece que ele tenta compensar a fraqueza externa com uma ideologia de força, ou é uma força de destruição.

É aquele típico pai que se diz Cristão, mas é violento, quebrou seu casamento, afastou seus filhos, é sozinho e vive de gritar e xingar suas asneiras por aí. Você conhece um, aposto.

É muito mais proveitoso pessoalmente defender estatismo e socialismo, pegar os frutos disso, e ser um hipócrita vivendo a liberdade e capitalismo que os libertários construíram. 

E isso cria um exército profissional de defensores do estado. Milhões deles. Funcionários públicos de alto escalão, professores de universidade de cursos inúteis que nem dão aula, chefes de sindicatos e ONGs, políticos, jornalistas, artistas.

Isso não é novidade. O Rothbard descreveu essa gente em vários dos seus escritos.

Mas é esse desbalanço entre incentivos que leva a existência de um exército de estatistas, e em boa parte o que leva a situação que temos hoje no mundo.

Quem tem os valores libertários, seja instintivamente, por criação, ou por educação, está fazendo o que é melhor pra ele individualmente. Quem tem valores de parasita, vagabundo, autoritário ou qualquer mistura disso, faz carreira promovendo isso.

Abençoado seja quem conseguir resolver esse problema de incentivos.

kept.text:
🚨 Why does @SpaceX exist? Because @elonmusk was the only one crazy enough for space at the time 🔥

🎙️ Elon explains that this "craziness" was actually born from a simple, frustrating question: How did we end up with supercomputers in our pockets but no way to get off the planet?

"If you had asked people in 1969 what the world would look like in fifty years, they would have expected a base on the moon and some people visiting Mars. Maybe even a base on Mars. They would expect orbiting space hotels and other awesome stuff.

Instead, if you told them, 'Well, we have a device smaller than a deck of cards with access to all the world's information, and you can talk to anyone instantly on planet Earth. But the United States will not be able to send anyone to orbit,' they would have called bullshit.

They'd say, 'You have all of that, and nothing is happening in space?!'

After we sold PayPal in 2001, I was trying to figure out exactly why that was the case. The origin of SpaceX was trying to figure out why we had not sent anyone to Mars. The obvious next step after Apollo was to send people to Mars. Every year I’d look at the NASA website, and there didn’t seem to be a date for it.

We were able to go to the moon in 1969. Our last mission to the moon was in 1972. Now here we are half a century later and we still have not gone back.

It would be extremely tragic if Apollo was the high-water mark for humanity, if the moon was as far as we got. Yet, the space shuttle could only take people to low earth orbit. Then the space shuttle retired, and the United States could take no one to orbit. The trend was dwindling down to nothing.

Does this mean we've peaked as a civilization?

That thought is why the original idea for SpaceX wasn’t to create a company. It was simply to figure out why we hadn’t sent people to Mars. At first, I thought maybe we’d lost the will to explore. I thought we had to create the will to explore. But that was wrong.

We have not lost the will to explore; people just did not think there was a way forward.

If people don’t think there’s a way, then they won’t continuously bash their head against the wall for progress. However, once people realized, 'There is a way to do this,' we got a lot of support.

It showed me that there must be things to inspire us—that make you proud to be a member of humanity. The Apollo moon landing was an example of that. Only a handful of people went to the moon—and yet actually we all went to the moon. We went with them vicariously. We shared in that adventure.

No one would say that was a bad idea—that Apollo wasn’t great. We need more of those. Ultimately, the United States is a distillation of the human spirit of exploration. It's fundamental to the psyche.

Taking on this challenge just required a certain mindset. I’ve always been optimistic. If I wasn’t optimistic, I wouldn’t be attempting all these crazy things. I must be pathologically optimistic, I suppose."

Over 300 million people on Earth today have literally zero cell signal - no towers, no infrastructure, nothing

Traditional telecom simply can’t reach them

For decades, the telecom industry’s answer was: “build more towers”

That works great if you live in a city. Not so helpful if you’re in rural Africa, the Amazon, or out in the ocean

That’s what makes Starlink Direct-to-Cell so important

Starlink is turning every tower on Earth into something optional

SpaceX now has 650+ satellites acting as cell towers, orbiting about 340 miles above Earth

And they connect directly to regular LTE phones - no dish, no special handset, no new hardware

Texting and basic data (including internet calls) are already live with T-Mobile and One NZ. Traditional cellular voice and high-speed data are next, with more global carriers lining up

kept.text:
NEWS: SpaceX successfully launches Viasat's third satellite, ViaSat-3 F3, completing the rival constellation aimed at competing with Starlink.

The Falcon Heavy rocket lifted off from Kennedy Space Center in Florida at 10:13 a.m., two days after the original launch date, which was pushed back due to weather.

Five hours after liftoff, the satellite separated from the rocket and ground control confirmed it had reached a healthy orbit. The satellite will spend the next four months traveling to its final position 22,000 miles above the Asia-Pacific region.

Once in place, the satellite will deploy its antenna and deliver 1 terabit per second of capacity. This will double the bandwidth of Viasat's entire fleet in 2026.

The launch is a major win for Viasat after a rough start. The first $700 million satellite failed when its antenna didn't fully open in orbit, cutting coverage across the Americas and sending the company's stock down nearly 30%.

The second and third satellites have worked as planned. Viasat shares climbed 2.1% on launch day, closing at $59.28.

Despite the win for Viasat, the bigger picture still favors Starlink. SpaceX already operates over 6,000 satellites in low Earth orbit serving 12+ million users globally, while Viasat's constellation is just three satellites in much higher orbit.

kept.text:
How @SpaceX Threads New Satellites Into a Moving Constellation

A @Starlink launch does not simply throw satellites into their final homes. The upper stage inserts them low, the stack releases as a compact train, and each spacecraft later uses onboard propulsion to raise orbit, spread out, and phase into its assigned slot. 

SpaceX describes this as a staged mission profile of insertion, parking orbit, orbit raise, on-station operations, and eventually deorbit.

The safety story is also part of the geometry. SpaceX says Starlink satellites are deployed into low insertion orbits below 400 km, so early failures decay quickly, and the satellites use autonomous collision avoidance with updated tracking data for close approaches.

quoted_tweet.text:
Marc Andreessen just revealed how Harvard Business School was built on a broken 1941 theory, and how it's now collapsing...

Andreessen co-founded Netscape in 1994 and a16z in 2009.

He has sat on Meta's board since 2008.

He has spent 30 years backing founders and watching managerial CEOs lose to them.

The pattern traces back to one book: James Burnham's *The Machiavellians* (1941).

Burnham argued every great company had been founder-run.

Henry Ford ran Ford. Bob Noyce ran Intel.

Today, Elon Musk runs Tesla, SpaceX, and Starlink.

Then, he said, something broke.

Between the 1880s and the 1920s, a new philosophy replaced the founder. It was called managerialism.

The professional manager would now hold a portable skill, usable across any business.

The consequences were:

- Harvard Business School
- Stanford Business School
- Management as a universal skill
- The 1970s conglomerate

"That assumes the managers are going to do a good job," Andreessen says.

For 30 years, they haven't.

Managers can run something static, he says. Soup is soup. A bank is a bank. A car is a car.

But when the industry changes, the manager freezes.

Look at SpaceX.

"Imagine being a professionally trained manager, trained at a top management school, working for a rocket launch company, competing with SpaceX."

Then Elon's rockets started landing on their butt.

"Your management skills ... what good are they at that point?"

Andreessen's conclusion:

"You're much more likely to build something important in the 21st century if you start with the founder and train them on management."

What "professionally run" institution in your life has quietly stopped working?

🗺️ La trajectoire prévue est consultable sur notre carte. Les observateurs situés à proximité de la ligne centrale (ligne jaune) pourront bénéficier d’un passage proche du zénith, avec une trajectoire orientée du nord-ouest vers le sud-est. En s’éloignant de cet axe, l’élévation apparente diminuera progressivement vers l’horizon. 

👀 La trajectoire est plus au sud que le passage de vendredi : l’observation sera donc plus favorable dans le sud-ouest de la France et la nord-est de l’Espagne.

⚠️ La visibilité reste conditionnée à plusieurs paramètres, notamment les conditions d’éclairement solaire en altitude, l’orientation des panneaux des satellites ainsi que la météo locale. Ces facteurs pourraient réduire ou empêcher la perception du train.

$ASTS: I’ve been periodically posting Google Trends results for @AST_SpaceMobile in January, May, and December of 2025 and now, to better understand how rapidly the company is becoming of interest to the world. The charts below start when Abel founded AST in May 2017. 

While the stock price has been going down lately, worldwide search interest for ASTS continues to go up; i.e., the relative popularity of ASTS is increasing.
 
Over the past year, ASTS search interest has been on an uptrend generally matching Starlink for “news” category searches and more frequently exceeding Starlink for “finance” searches.

According to an analysis of the data by Gemini AI, for "news" searches “by 2026 the public and investors are now viewing ASTS as a major peer or competitor to Starlink in the satellite communications space” and for "finance" searches "ASTS is no longer the obscure underdog; it now frequently captures more search attention than Starlink during key news cycles".

SpaceX searches are accelerating likely because of the pending IPO.
